xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:AddressOfRegisteredOfficeOfCom: ICICI Bank Tower, Near Chakli Circle, Old Padra Road, Vadodara, Gujarat 390007
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:AddressOfCorporateOfficeOfComp: ICICI Bank Towers, Bandra-Kurla Complex, Mumbai 400051
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NotesGeneralDisclosureExplanat:
Footnotes to Section A
Question 21(a)
1. Includes employees of all locations, except subsidiaries; excludes employees on deputation to other entities. 2. ’Other than permanent’ includes interns and employees on fixed term contracts.
Question 21(b) 1. ’Other than permanent’ includes interns and employees on fixed term contracts. 2. Basis the voluntary disclosures made by the employees.
Question 22 1. Excludes employees on deputation to other entities.
Question 24 1. Effective March 24, 2025, ICICI Securities was delisted from stock exchanges and it became a wholly-owned subsidiary of the Bank. 2. ICICI Securities Holdings, Inc. is a wholly owned subsidiary of ICICI Securities Limited. 3. ICICI Securities, Inc. is a wholly owned subsidiary of ICICI Securities Holdings, Inc. 4. ICICI Prudential Pension Funds Management Company Limited is a wholly owned subsidiary of ICICI Prudential Life Insurance Company Limited. 5. The entity is a joint venture company, however, it is considered as a subsidiary company in accordance with the provisions of the Companies Act, 2013. 6. On June 27, 2025, the Bank approved purchase of up to 2% additional shareholding in the ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited (Pru AMC). This purchase will primarily be towards maintaining the Bank’s majority shareholding in the event of grant of stock-based compensation by Pru AMC, subject to receipt of requisite approvals. 7. These companies are not considered as associates in the financial statements, in accordance with the provisions of AS 23 on ‘Accounting for Investments in Associates in Consolidated Financial Statements’. 8. Acquired pursuant to debt settlement 9. The Bank executed a share purchase agreement for sale of its entire shareholding in the equity shares of FISERV Merchant Solutions Private Limited on March 29, 2025. The share transfer was completed subsequently on April 17, 2025 and it ceased to be an associate of the Bank effective that day. 10. On June 11, 2025, the Bank executed a share purchase agreement for sale of its entire shareholding in the equity shares of NIIT Institute of Finance Banking and Insurance Training Limited, consequent to which the share transfer was completed and it ceased to be an associate of the Bank effective the same day.
Question 27 1. The Bank re-examined its material topics in FY2025 and conducted an ESG materiality survey to identify areas that were considered important by our stakeholders.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:RemarksForAssuranceOfDetailsOf: Reasonable assurance has been done on 1.c.
Reasonable assurance has been done on 3.b.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DescriptionOfBusinessActivity: Banking activities by Central, Commercial and Saving banks
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:ProductOrServiceSoldByTheEntit: Monetary intermediation of commercial banks, saving banks, postal saving banks and discount houses
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:ABriefOnTypesOfCustomersExplan: The Bank services a wide range of customers including corporates, micro, small and medium enterprises, salaried and self-employed individuals, senior citizens, farmers, non-resident Indians and others.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NameOfTheHoldingOrSubsidiaryAs: ICICI Venture Funds Management Company Limited
ICICI Prudential Pension Funds Management Company Limited4
I-Process Services (India) Limited (formerly known as I-Process Services (India) Private Limited)
ICICI Lombard General Insurance Company Limited
ICICI Prudential Life Insurance Company Limited
ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited5,6
Rajasthan Asset Management Company Private Limited7
FISERV Merchant Solutions Private Limited (erstwhile ICICI Merchant Services Private Limited)9
NIIT Institute of Finance Banking and Insurance Training Limited10
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:WebLinkForGrievanceRedressPoli: Any queries or complaints can be shared by sending an email to concerns@icicifoundation.org
Any queries or complaints can be shared by sending an email to ICICIbonds@3i-infotech.com
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:RemarksStakeHolderGroupFromWho: All pending complaints have been disposed off after the close of the year
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:WebLinkForGrievanceRedressPoli: Any queries or complaints can be shared by sending an email to einward.ris@kfintech.com or companysecretary@icicibank.com
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:RemarksStakeHolderGroupFromWho: Out of 15 complaints, three complaints were pending closure with the statutory authority.
All pending complaints have been disposed off after the close of the year
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:WebLinkForGrievanceRedressPoli: Available internally on the Bank’s intranet
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:RemarksStakeHolderGroupFromWho: Complaints received during the year were closed within the applicable turnaround time
Complaints received during the year were closed within the applicable turnaround time
Out of 32 complaints, 8 cases were pending
All pending complaints were subsequently closed.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:RationaleForIdentifyingTheRisk: Failure to serve customers with appropriate product offering or value-added services, or inappropriate conduct can lead to loss of trust and risk the reputation of the Bank.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:InCaseOfRiskApproachToAdaptOrM: The Bank’s philosophy of ‘Fair to Customer, Fair to Bank’ emphasises the need to deliver fair value to customers. There is emphasis on transparency in engagements and offering simple products and value added techenabled services. Clear and accurate communication of products and services enable customers to take sound decisions about their financial needs. The Board-level Customer Service Committee oversees all aspects relating to customers, including having inperson interactions with various types of customers. Undertaking activities that are in the interest of customers also forms part of the Risk and Compliance Culture policy of the Bank and is monitored by an executive level forum with an annual reporting to the Audit Committee as well as Risk Committee of the Board.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:RationaleForIdentifyingTheRisk: Banking is a business based on trust and requires appropriate conduct when engaging with customers. Generating business while protecting the interests of customers contributes to attracting customers and growth in business.

Employees are critical in shaping a Bank’s ethical standards, build trust with customers, developing customer-centric solutions and embedding a culture of inclusivity and sustainability. Low employee engagement or dissatisfaction can lead to high turnover, reduced productivity and reputation risks. Human capital is important for the long-term sustainable performance of the Bank.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:InCaseOfRiskApproachToAdaptOrM: The Bank is an equal opportunity employer. Regular employee engagement initiatives and inclusive workplace ensure a diverse, equitable and non-discriminatory work environment. Through continuous investment in skill development and leadership programmes, the Bank enhances the capabilities of employees to serve customers effectively and contribute to its long-term success. Employee well-being measures are given due importance as part of HR practices.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:RationaleForIdentifyingTheRisk: Employees are ambassadors of the Bank and are key to identifying client needs and offering appropriate products and services. Cross-functional collaboration and the ethos of ‘One Bank, One Team’ and embedding the Bank’s Risk and Compliance Culture policy ensure responsible business practices for the Bank.

The impact of climate change poses tangible risks to the Bank’s own operations as well as business resilience of its borrowers. The consequences of climate change include both incremental effects (a long-term change in the mean and variability of climate pattern) and acute effects (increase in frequency and severity of extreme weather event). Assessing physical and transition risks are important to understand the impact of climate change events that can be felt across the Bank’s own operations as well as business models of its borrowers.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:InCaseOfRiskApproachToAdaptOrM: The Bank has a business continuity management and disaster recovery policy that details on actions to be considered in case of extreme climate events and conducts periodic assessment of climate risk on the infrastructure including data centres and selected operation hubs. With regard to its lending portfolio, the Bank has implemented a Social and Environmental Management Framework which provides the basis for screening new project finance proposals for social and environmental risks. The Bank has also incorporated ESG and climate change related parameters as part of the credit evaluation process for all lending proposals. A Climate Risk Management Framework has been formulated that lays down the process for scenario analysis and stress testing of the Bank’s loans and advances portfolio, due to climate risk events. The climate scenario analysis enables to quantify the impact of climate-related financial risks and assess the potential impact on capital and provisions requirement.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:RationaleForIdentifyingTheRisk: Significant opportunities are likely to emerge as efforts to meet commitments towards sustainable growth and transition to become carbon neutral or net zero gain traction in the country. The Bank has been supporting sustainable and sustainability-linked projects in areas like renewable energy, electric vehicles, green buildings, sanitation, waste management, and new emerging areas like green hydrogen based on an appropriate risk-return assessment.

Some policies are available on the Bank’s website: https://www.icicibank.com/ Some policies are only available on the Bank’s intranet.
Some policies are available on the Bank’s website: https://www.icicibank.com/ Some policies are only available on the Bank’s intranet.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:SpecificCommitmentsGoalsAndTar: The Bank has set itself a goal of becoming carbon neutral in Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions by 2032
The Bank has set itself a goal of becoming carbon neutral in Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions by 2032
The Bank has set itself a goal of becoming carbon neutral in Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions by 2032
The Bank has set itself a goal of becoming carbon neutral in Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions by 2032
The Bank has set itself a goal of becoming carbon neutral in Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions by 2032
The Bank has set itself a goal of becoming carbon neutral in Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions by 2032
The Bank has set itself a goal of becoming carbon neutral in Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions by 2032
The Bank has set itself a goal of becoming carbon neutral in Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions by 2032
The Bank has set itself a goal of becoming carbon neutral in Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions by 2032
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:StatementByDirectorResponsible: Statement by Executive Director included in the ESG Report for FY2025
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NotesManagementAndProcessDiscl: 1. The Bank’s contracts with value chain partners requires them to provide confirmation that they are compliant with applicable human rights requirements including labour laws. 2. United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights (UNGP) and International Labour Organization’s Declaration on Fundamental Principles and Rights at Work. 3. During FY2025, ISO 45001:2018 certification has been extended to twenty-one large offices of the Bank as compared to eighteen in FY2024. 4. ICICI Bank has adopted security policies including cyber security, regulatory compliance, risk management etc. based on inputs from extant global frameworks like NIST, ISO 27001 guidelines of Gopalakrishna Committee of RBI, RBI security standards of 2016 and inputs from internal and external audits to instill confidence and integrity of the organisation. 5. The Bank periodically undertakes independent assessment/evaluation of the working of its policies by external agencies. The disclosure pertains to assessments undertaken in FY2025.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:TopicsOrPrinciplesCoveredUnder: Programmes covered topics like governance, IT and cyber security, climate change risk sustainability, Artificial Intelligence and engagement with business leaders in other sectors
The programmes focused on areas like compliance by conscience, code of conduct, risk and compliance culture, anti-money laundering. The programmes were well received by the participants.
The programmes focused on areas like compliance by conscience, code of conduct, risk and compliance culture, anti-money laundering. The programmes were well received by the participants.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:BriefOfTheMonetaryCaseForPenal: The penalty was imposed on May 21, 2024, for deficiencies in regulatory compliance
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:BriefOfTheMonetaryCaseForCompo: Compounding order issued on October 16, 2024 for delay in reporting of stock options granted to non-resident employees during the period September 2015 to May 2018
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:AntiCorruptionOrAntiBriberyPol: Yes, the Bank has an Anti-Bribery and Anti-Corruption Policy. The Bank takes a zero-tolerance approach to bribery and corruption and is committed to act professionally, fairly and with integrity in all relationships and business dealings wherever it operates and to implementing and enforcing effective systems to counter bribery.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfTheEntityHaveProcesse: The Bank has adopted a Framework for Managing Conflict of Interest which articulates measures to ensure that conflicts of interest are handled in an appropriate manner, at the individual employee level, at the level of Board of Directors and at the Group level. The members of the Board provide declaration on an annual basis as well as when there is any change during the year regarding the entities they are interested in and the Bank ensures requisite approvals, as required under the statute as well as the Bank’s policies, are in place before transacting with such entities/individuals. Moreover, the Directors are required to recuse themselves from discussions involving any conflict of interest. The Directors need to exercise their responsibilities in a bonafide manner in the interest of the Bank. Weblink to the policy: https://www.icicibank.com/about-us/other-policies
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NotesPrinciple1ExplanatoryText:
Footnote for Principle 1
Essential
Question 1 1. In addition to the above, employees had also taken up e-learning programmes on several functional and skill development topics like personal data protection, information security, Code of Conduct, among others. 2. Overall, the Bank had delivered an average of about 10 learning days of training per employee in FY2025.
Question 8 1. Comprises vendor payments forming part of Bills Payable and Sundry Creditors under Schedule 5 of the audited financial statement. 2. For FY2025, the approach for the calculation was modified to better reflect the Bank’s accounting practices and the guidance provided by SEBI’s Industry Standards Forum. Basis the modified approach, the corresponding number for FY2024 was 11 days.
Question 9 1. These are not applicable for banking considering the nature of business. 2. As per the guidance provided by SEBI’s Industry Standards Forum, the FY2025 disclosure for related party transactions is on the basis of SEBI LODR Regulations.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfImprovementsInEnviron: Replacement of ACs, diesel generator sets, chillers, etc. that are energy efficient/star rated, solar energy equipment and BIFMA certified furniture
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DescribeTheProcessesInPlaceToS: The Bank does not have any products that create hazardous waste or e-waste. The Bank’s endeavour is to digitally provide products and services. The Bank may generate paper and plastics waste when providing products like credit/debit cards and cheque books to customers, which are not reclaimed by the Bank.

The Bank does not have any products that create hazardous waste or e-waste. The Bank’s endeavour is to digitally provide products and services. The Bank may generate paper and plastics waste when providing products like credit/debit cards and cheque books to customers, which are not reclaimed by the Bank.

The Bank does not have any products that create hazardous waste or e-waste. The Bank’s endeavour is to digitally provide products and services. The Bank may generate paper and plastics waste when providing products like credit/debit cards and cheque books to customers, which are not reclaimed by the Bank.

The Bank does not have any products that create hazardous waste or e-waste. The Bank’s endeavour is to digitally provide products and services. The Bank may generate paper and plastics waste when providing products like credit/debit cards and cheque books to customers, which are not reclaimed by the Bank.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:TheEntityHasNotApplicableCondu: Note: LCAs for cheque book and credit card were conducted during FY2023; no LCA was conducted in FY2024 and FY2025.

Essential
Question 1 BIFMA: Business + Institutional Furniture Manufacturers Association. 1. Details of improvements pertain to FY2025.
Leadership

Question 4 Note: This assessment was not undertaken.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:IndicateInPutMaterial: FSC Recycled Pre-Print Forms as % of total pre-print forms
FSC Recycled Pre-Print Forms as % of total pre-print forms
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:WebLinkOfEqualOppertunityPolic:
The Bank is committed to adoption of fair employment practices. It works towards diversity at workplace through efforts to recruit, develop and retain talented people from a diverse candidate pool. It upholds the principle that advancement is based on performance and there is a commitment to equal opportunity. This is mentioned in the Group Code of Business Conduct and Ethics. The Bank’s Diversity, Equity and Inclusion Policy also emphasizes maintaining a culture of no discrimination.
The Group Code of Business Conduct and Ethics is available at: https://www.icicibank.com/content/dam/icicibank/india/managed-assets/revamp-page-images/docs/pdf/code_of_business_conduct_ethics.pdf?ITM=nli_home_na_footer_4_CMS_other_businessConductAndEthics_NLI
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfMechanismAvailableToR:
Yes. A system is available internally for employees to raise grievance including issues pertaining to women safety and improper behaviour at workplace.
The grievance, once raised, is duly investigated and appropriate action is taken as per the organizational policy.

A system is available internally for employees to raise grievance including issues pertaining to women safety and improper behaviour at workplace.
The grievance, once raised, is duly investigated and appropriate action is taken as per the organizational policy.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfOccupationalHealthAnd: ISO 45001:2018 Occupational Health and Safety Standard is being adopted at the Bank. During FY2025, three new locations were added, taking the total to 21 premises like large offices, call centres, data centres, learning centre and staff quarters.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DescribeTheMeasuresTakenByTheE: ICICI Bank has implemented various measures to ensure a safe & healthy workplace. Some of these are: 1. Safety training: Employees are provided comprehensive safety training to educate them about potential fire hazards and ensuring correct use of safety equipment in case of emergency. First-aid training is also provided to reduce and prevent workplace injuries. 2. Periodic checks: Periodic inspections of the workplace by Tower Managers/Infrastructure Facility Managers/Zonal Safety & Security Managers are carried out to identify and address any safety hazards. 3. Safety equipment: The Bank’s premises have appropriate safety equipment, such as fire alarm system, portable fire extinguishers, sprinklers, fire hydrants, smoke mask, wheelchairs, staircase evacuation chairs. All safety equipment are periodically inspected as part of service maintenance contract to ensure its functionality. 4. Emergency plans: Clear procedures including fire drills and evacuation plans have been established for responding to emergencies at Towers, Large offices & Business centres. 5. Health programmes: Under the ‘#Befit360’ initiative, various wellness programmes and resources are being provided to promote physical health and mental well-being among employees. 6. Safety committee: Safety committees have been formed to involve employees in identifying hazards and identify hazard mitigation plan. 7. Compliance with regulation: The Bank ensures compliance with relevant health and safety regulations and standards set by regulatory authorities, as applicable to the Bank. 8. Communication: On an ongoing basis, email communications & advisories are circulated to maintain a healthy workplace environment. 9. Continual improvement: The relevant teams regularly review and update safety policies and procedures to adapt to changing conditions and incorporate best practices.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NotesPrinciple3ExplanatoryText:
Footnotes for Principle 3
Essential Question 1(a) 1. All employees are covered but eligibility shall be as per The Maternity Benefit (Amendment) Act, 2017. 2. ‘Other than permanent’ includes interns and employees on fixed term contracts.
Question 1(c) 1. Disclosure for FY2025 is as per the guidance provided by SEBI’s Industry Standards Forum.
Question 5 Note: Not applicable for male employees.
Question 6 1. ‘Other than permanent’ includes Interns and employees on Fixed Term Contracts.
Question 7 1. Excludes employees on deputation to other entities.
Question 8 1. Employees with zero mandays are considered untrained. 2. Only permanent employees considered; excludes employees on deputation to other entities.
Question 9 1. Excludes employees who joined in the month of March. 2. Excludes employees who joined in the month of February and March. 3. Excludes employees on deputation to other entities. 4. Excludes interns and employees on fixed term contracts
Question 11 1. During FY2025, there were five injuries involving staff of agencies providing services to the Bank. These were not directly related to performance of work-related task. The Bank has taken necessary action to support the injured and suitable corrective actions have been taken, where required.
Question 14 Note: Of the total business centres, 1,408 were assessed by third party during FY2025.
Leadership
Question 3 Note: The three affected employees did not suffer any high consequence work-related injury/ill-health/fatalities. The employees returned to work and did not require any kind of rehabilitation.
Question 5 Note: 185 vendors were assessed during FY2025 under the Bank’s Supplier Assessment Framework.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfMeasuresUndertakenByT: The Bank has put in place a process of seeking confirmation on payment of statutory dues from its manpower sourcing vendors. The relevant evidence cannot be more than two months old.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DescribeTheProcessesForIdentif: The Bank considers as stakeholder all individuals/entities like customers, investors, employees, regulators and communities with whom it engages in conducting its business. The Bank has a long-standing commitment towards socio-economic development of the underprivileged and also treats the society as an important stakeholder.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:ProvideTheProcessesForConsulta: The Bank has various Board committees that oversee the processes and mechanisms for engagement with key stakeholders. There are dedicated teams that engage with respective stakeholders like customers, investors and regulators on an ongoing basis, and provide feedback to the Board committees during quarterly meetings. There may also be in-person meetings of these stakeholders with Board members. As part of the Corporate Social Responsibility initiatives, the Bank, through its philanthropic arm, ICICI Foundation for Inclusive Growth, engages with local authorities and makes efforts to understand critical requirements or interventions required at grassroot level to promote sustainable ecosystems. Updates are regularly provided to the CSR Committee.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfInstancesAsToHowTheIn: The engagement by the Bank’s teams with stakeholders like employees, investors, borrowers, regulators, industry bodies and communities provides relevant inputs on environment and social topics. These are factored into the ESG initiatives undertaken by the Bank.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfInstancesOfEngagement: The Bank has an ongoing engagement with customers from vulnerable groups, particularly in the rural and business banking segment, to meet their financial requirements. Further, as part of corporate social responsibility, several social initiatives are being undertaken across India. Please refer to the Environmental, Social and Governance Report of FY2025 for relevant case studies.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfOtherChannelsOfCommun: For details, refer to the Social and Relationship Capital chapter of the Integrated Report.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:PurposeAndScopeOfEngagementInc: For details, refer to the Social and Relationship Capital chapter of the Integrated Report.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfOtherChannelsOfCommun: For details, refer to the Social and Relationship Capital chapter of the Integrated Report.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:PurposeAndScopeOfEngagementInc: For details, refer to the Social and Relationship Capital chapter of the Integrated Report.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfOtherChannelsOfCommun: For details, refer to the Social and Relationship Capital chapter of the Integrated Report.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:PurposeAndScopeOfEngagementInc: For details, refer to the Social and Relationship Capital chapter of the Integrated Report.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfOtherChannelsOfCommun: For details, refer to the Social and Relationship Capital chapter of the Integrated Report.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:PurposeAndScopeOfEngagementInc: For details, refer to the Social and Relationship Capital chapter of the Integrated Report.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfOtherChannelsOfCommun: For details, refer to the Social and Relationship Capital chapter of the Integrated Report.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:PurposeAndScopeOfEngagementInc: For details, refer to the Social and Relationship Capital chapter of the Integrated Report.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DescribeTheInternalMechanismsI:
The Bank has in place the Group Code of Business Conduct and Ethics, Whistleblower Policy, and Policy against Sexual Harassment at workplace, which have set mechanisms to redress grievances related to, and not limited to, human rights issues. Additionally, the Bank has the Human Rights Policy and Diversity, Equity and Inclusion Policy which states the commitment of ICICI Bank to promote and respect human rights and promote and nurture a culture of diversity, equity and inclusion.
Group Code of Business Conduct and Ethics: Any breach of the Code of Conduct (which includes workplace concerns and safety), is treated as misconduct. In cases where staff accountability has to be examined, the disciplinary process is carried out. Disciplinary procedure would start after detailed fact finding exercise/internal investigation including one-to-one discussion with the concerned employee, where-ever possible-by the respective business group and a detailed report to that effect is submitted to the human resources management group for taking the necessary action. Such investigation is carried out independent of the respective business group.
Whistle Blower Policy: An employee may send communication through e-mail to chairman.auditcommittee@icicibank.com or head.whistleblower@icicibank.com. The employee may also send a communication directly in writing through a letter to Chairman, Audit Committee or to General Counsel of ICICI Bank Limited. Additional modes of communication or access may be made available to employees in appropriate or exceptional situations.
Policy against Sexual Harassment at workplace: To report a complaint, an employee can write to the secretary of the management committee, or raise a Service Request on the Bank’s online portal available on the intranet. The secretary will then constitute an investigation committee to take the investigation forward.
Human Rights Policy: The policy underlines ICICI Bank’s commitment to promote and respect human rights and aims to provide a work environment that respects and upholds individual dignity. The Human Rights Policy of the organization is aligned to the United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights (UNGP) and International Labour Organization’s Declaration on Fundamental Principles and Rights at Work. ICICI Bank believes in the corporate responsibility in promoting and safeguarding internationally proclaimed human rights. The Human Rights Policy aims to create awareness, promote and redress grievances in the following areas – Safe and Harassment Free Workplace, Free from Sexual Harassment, Diversity and Inclusion, Freedom of Expression, Free from Child and Forced Labour.
Equal Opportunity: The Bank’s commitment to nurturing and promoting a culture of diversity, equity and inclusion enables employees to work effectively, free from any biases. The objective of the diversity, equity and inclusion (DE&I) policy is to:
• Promote a culture of non-discrimination across the organization that percolates to every employee • Ensure that the Bank continues to be an employer for diverse groups • Maintain an environment of inclusion for all its employees • Maintain a culture of no discrimination. The above policies are available in the Bank’s intranet, which is accessible to all employees.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:RemarksforComplaintsExplanator: Complaints received during the year were closed within the applicable turnaround time
Complaints received during the year were closed within the applicable turnaround time
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:MechanismsToPreventAdverseCons:
The Bank is committed to protection of the complainant in discrimination and harassment cases. In cases covered under the Policy against Sexual Harassment at workplace, the complainant is provided protection as under: • Proceed on paid leave till closure of investigation (on request by the complainant). • Temporary posting to another branch/office till closure of investigation (on request by the complainant). • 100% confidentiality on the case details, complainant and respondent.
In whistle blowing cases, the identity of the whistle blower is not revealed. The Bank also does not tolerate any attempt on the part of anyone to retaliate, to cause disadvantage or to discriminate against any person who has reported to the Bank serious and genuine concerns regarding an apparent wrong-doing. Protection under the policy is available to the employee who raises the concern under this policy till such time that the complainant’s employment subsists with the Bank or its subsidiaries.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NotesPrinciple5ExplanatoryText:
Footnotes to Principle 5 Essential
Question 1 1. ’Other than permanent’ includes interns and employees on fixed term contracts. 2. Excludes employees on deputation to other entities.
Question 2 Note: Minimum wages are not applicable for the Bank.
Question 3(a) 1. Remuneration is of Non-executive Directors (NEDs) and includes only fixed remuneration. The sitting fees for attending the meetings of Board and Board committees is ` 100,000 per meeting which is not mentioned above. 2. Median remuneration for KMP is calculated considering the fixed remuneration along with perquisites. 3. Median remuneration of permanent staff (excluding overseas staff) is calculated considering the fixed remuneration along with perquisites. The median of grade-wise median is shown above.
Question 3(b) 1. Disclosure for FY2025 is as per the guidance provided by SEBI’s Industry Standards Forum. 2. Data pertains to operations in India.
Question 7 1. In FY2025, average female headcount has been considered as per guidance provided by SEBI’s Industry Standards Forum. 2. FY2024 numbers are not restated as per the revised guidance by SEBI’s Industry Standards Forum. 3. Data pertains to operations in India.
Leadership
Question 4 Note: The Bank’s relevant team periodically assesses its value chain partners on various ESG parameters which include some of the above. During FY2025, declaration was sought from 185 value chain partners relating to some of the above points.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfProjectRelatedToReduc: The Bank has set a target to become carbon neutral in Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions by FY2032. Various initiatives are being evaluated and assessed as part of the roadmap to achieve the target. The Bank is also in the process of onboarding a digital tool to improve ESG data governance, strengthening carbon emission calculations across all categories and monitoring emission reduction strategies.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfWasteManagementPracti: ICICI Bank’s commitment to sustainability extends to efficient management of all waste generated at its facilities. We have in place a robust process for handling waste, including composting organic waste in large premises. We also have a process in place to manage e-waste, wherein we hand over the waste for recycling to authorised agencies in compliance with various national and local regulations on waste management. ICICI Bank has introduced several measures to enable waste reduction and recycling at its BKC Service Centre (Corporate office) in Mumbai, Maharashtra. This includes proper waste segregation at the site for suitable treatment according to the type of waste. The Bank has also advocated the use of environment-friendly consumables at the premises with the aim of promoting sustainable processes.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DisclosureWebLinkOfEntityAtWhi: The Bank has a defined Business Continuity Management (BCM) (including disaster management) plan to ensure continuity of critical services to customers and availability of critical systems during significant disruptions under defined disaster scenarios. In the event of a disaster, the Bank endeavours to resume business and operations to an acceptable level as per the defined Recovery Time Objectives (RTO) detailed in the plan. The RTO provides guidance for prioritizing activities, whenever necessary. There can be external factors beyond the Bank’s control, which could affect the actual RTO. The BCM plans are reviewed and maintained to incorporate changes in the organization or environment and are subject to modification based on the reviews, situations, circumstances, etc. The efficacy of the BCM plan is established through periodic business continuity testing. The Bank also has a Disaster Recovery (DR) plan to ensure continuity of critical services to customers and availability of identified critical systems in the event of disaster (IT system disruption). The DR plans for the identified critical applications are reviewed and maintained to incorporate changes in the application or environment and are subject to modification based on the reviews. The efficacy of the DR plan is established through periodic DR testing by way of DR drills as per approved annual DR calendar.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NotesPrinciple6ExplanatoryText:
Footnotes for Principle 6
Essential
Question 1 1. Includes electricity consumption through on-site solar capacity. 2. The Bank purchased International Renewable Energy Certificates equivalent to 11,000 MWh in FY2025. Including I-RECs, the proportion of renewable energy in total energy consumption was 34.3% in FY2025. 3. For FY2025, PPP conversion factor for India is Rs. 20.66 per international Dollar based on International Monetary Fund data. This is in accordance with the guidance provided by SEBI’s Industry Standards Forum. 4. For FY2024, PPP conversion factor of Rs. 20.96 per international Dollar was based on the World Bank's data. 5. Data pertains to operations in India.
Question 3 1. Data for FY2024 is estimated based on actual water withdrawal tracked at 32 premises of the Bank and extrapolated for all locations. 2. Data for FY2025 is estimated based on the guidance provided by SEBI’s Industry Standards Forum, advising use of Central Ground Water Authority (CGWA) estimate for water consumption of 45 litres per head per working day for offices. This amount is recorded as water withdrawn from third party sources. 3. Water consumption for FY2025 is calculated based on Central Ground Water Authority (CGWA)’s guidelines of 25 litres per head per working day for offices. 4. For FY2025, PPP conversion factor for India is Rs. 20.66 per international Dollar based on IMF data. This is in accordance with the guidance provided by SEBI’s Industry Standards Forum. 5. For FY2024, PPP conversion factor of Rs. 20.96 per international Dollar was based on the World Bank's data.
Question 4 1. Water discharged has been calculated based on the guidance by the Central Ground Water Authority (CGWA) of estimated 20 litres per head per working day for offices.
Question 7 1. The Bank purchased International Renewable Energy Certificates (I-RECs) equivalent to 11,000 MWh (39,600 GJ) in FY2025. Including I-RECs, the estimated total Scope 2 emissions is 112,042.73 in FY2025. 2. For FY2025, PPP conversion factor for India is Rs. 20.66 per international Dollar based on IMF data. This is in accordance with the guidance provided by SEBI’s Industry Standards Forum. 3. For FY2024, PPP conversion factor of Rs. 20.96 per international Dollar was based on the World Bank's data. 4. For details on emission factors, refer the ESG Report 2024-25. 5. Data pertains to operations in India.
Question 9 1. Data pertains to all types of e-waste. 2. For FY2025, PPP conversion factor for India is Rs. 20.66 per international Dollar based on IMF data. This is in accordance with the guidance provided by SEBI’s Industry Standards Forum. 3. For FY2024, PPP conversion factor of Rs. 20.96 per international Dollar was based on World Bank data. 4. Reported basis the data provided by the e-waste disposal vendors.
Leadership
Question 2 1. Emissions for fiscal 2024 includes capital goods, business travel and employee commuting. 2. Emission for fiscal FY2025 includes capital goods, fuel-and-energy related activities, business travel, employee commuting, upstream/downstream transportation & distribution and waste generated in operations. 3. For details on emission factors, refer the ESG Report 2024-25.
Question 7 185 vendors were assessed during FY2025
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:InitiativeUndertaken: Sourcing green power from utilities at some locations by paying Green tariff
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:WebLinkForDetailsOfInitiativeT: Refer ESG Report for FY2025 available at https://www.icicibank.com/about-us/annual
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:OutcomeOfTheInitiative: Increase proportion of renewable energy in total energy consumption to reduce Scope 2 emissions
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:InitiativeUndertaken: Using FSC (Forest Stewardship Council) certified recycled paper for pre-print forms at business centres
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:WebLinkForDetailsOfInitiativeT: Refer ESG Report for FY2025 available at https://www.icicibank.com/about-us/annual
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:InitiativeUndertaken: Installed Atmospheric Water Generators (AWG) at five locations with cumulative capacity of 8,000 litres per day of potable water
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:WebLinkForDetailsOfInitiativeT: Refer ESG Report for FY2025 available at https://www.icicibank.com/about-us/annual
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:OutcomeOfTheInitiative: Reduce dependence on locally sourced packaged water for drinking purposes
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NameOfTheTradeAndIndustryChamb: Federation of Indian Chambers of Commerce and Industry (FICCI)
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:MethodResortedForSuchAdvocacy: Member of working group constituted by the Indian Banks’ Association
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DescribeTheMechanismsToReceive: Social initiatives of the Bank are largely conducted through ICICI Foundation for Inclusive Growth, which undertakes various environmental and social projects. ICICI Foundation has put in place a mechanism to receive complaints from communities on its website. Any queries or complaints can be shared by sending an email to concerns@icicifoundation.org.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NotesPrinciple8ExplanatoryText:
Footnote for Principle 8
Essential
Question 4 1. Calculation on cash payment basis.
Question 5 1. FY2025 numbers are as per the guidance provided by SEBI’s Industry Standards Forum. 2. Data pertains to operations in India.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DescribeTheMechanismsInPlaceTo: The Bank complies with the 'Customer Rights Policy' which enshrines the basic rights of customers including Right to Grievance Redress and Compensation. The Bank has made it easy and seamless for customers to raise complaints through multiple channels including the business centres, voice, and digital channels. The Bank has a Board approved Customer Grievance Redressal Policy which outlines the well-defined framework to address customer complaints ensuring fair, transparent, and timely resolution with clear turnaround times. All complaints are recorded in a Customer Relationship Management (CRM) system and tracked for end-to-end resolution. An escalation matrix built in the CRM system ensures that customer requirements are appropriately addressed within stipulated timelines. Detailed Root Cause Analysis (RCAs) of the issues highlighted in customer feedback, complaints, etc. are conducted and insights from the same are implemented to improve the products and processes and enhance services of the Bank. As recommended by the Reserve Bank of India, senior retired bankers are appointed as Internal Ombudsmen of the Bank. The Customer Service Committee of the Board, the Standing Committee on Customer Service and the business centre level Customer Service Committees monitor customer service at different levels.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:WebLinkOfThePolicyOnCyberSecur: The Bank has a Board approved cyber security policy which is available to employees on the Bank’s intranet portal. The Bank’s data privacy policy is available on the website at https://www.icicibank.com/privacy.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfAnyCorrectiveActionsT: The Bank has taken necessary corrective action to address the deficiencies in the regulatory compliance identified by the regulator.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:WeblinkWhereInformationOnProdu: Details about products and services offered by the Bank are available on the Bank’s website www.icicibank.com as well as on mobile banking platform.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:StepsTakenToInformAndEducateCo: The Bank aims to treat its customers fairly and provide transparency across its offerings. Continuous customer education efforts are made to enable customers to make informed choices regarding banking products and services. The Bank also seeks to ensure that the products offered are based on an assessment of the customer’s financial needs. The Bank regularly conducts customer awareness campaigns through social media, notifications on mobile apps, e-mails and SMS (in regional language also) regarding safe banking, cybersecurity as well as modus operandi of frauds prevalent in society. The Bank displays the awareness messages during new payee addition, high value fund transfer transactions to create awareness in the transaction flow. In addition to proactively undertaking customer awareness campaigns, the Bank creates awareness using other means like, • Safe Banking Tips - detailed ‘Do’s and Don’ts’ are updated on the website www.icicibank.com • Messages along with statement of accounts, physical and online • Messages printed on the Bank’s stationery and inserts in deliverables • SMS alerts • Message on phone banking when the customer calls • Posters at business centres and ATMs. The Bank also displays relevant messages at its offices and business centres for customer awareness. The Bank has been conducting extensive public awareness campaigns on fraud prevention on television and social media since FY2022. The #BeatTheCheat campaign spreads awareness on the modus operandi and pretext used by fraudsters to defraud the customers. The videos are made in 11 regional languages, and has been well-received. Topics covered various forms of fraud including fake lottery emails, fake QR codes, fake apps and parcel/digital arrest scam, providing scenarios and fraud prevention tips. Further, recognizing financial literacy as a need for customers’, the Bank introduced “The Orange Book”, a monthly e-magazine in English and Hindi languages that covers an array of financial topics from investment planning, safe banking to tax savings, financial preparedness and much more.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:MechanismsInPlaceToInformConsu: The Bank has a Customer Communication & Response Committee (CCRC) to manage customer communication and responsiveness of the Bank in case of system incidents. CCRC has representation from relevant internal stakeholder groups including customer service, business, channels, technology, corporate brand and communications, risk, credit monitoring, etc. Further, in the event of disruption or a disaster, the Bank has put in place mechanisms for customers to contact the Bank, details of which are also available on the Bank’s website.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfProductInformationOnT:
The Bank has installed LED screens at several business centres that display various messages relating to its products and services.
The Bank, on a continuous basis, measures the advocacy and satisfaction levels of customers across key products and touch points through the Net Promoter Score (NPS). As part of this exercise, the customers’ likelihood to recommend, satisfaction levels with the product/transaction experience and voice of customer (VOC) are measured.
Approximately, 3.8 million customers were covered for their feedback. This feedback is analysed and the insights are reviewed to improve the products and processes and enhance the services of the Bank. In addition, extensive diagnostic customer researches are conducted on a regular basis. The focus of such researches is to identify areas of improvement in the products and services of the Bank and arrive at appropriate action points.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NotesPrinciple9ExplanatoryText:
Footnote for Principle 9
Essential
Question 2 1. Pertains to revenue from retail digital banking business as a proportion of revenue from operations.
Question 3 1. The mechanism for receiving customer complaints was significantly enhanced across channels during FY2025, resulting in increase in the number of complaints received during the year.
